text: i had an early copy of Macromedia director. It was like $700 back then, came in a huge colorful box designed by Clement Mok who did some great packaging back in the day when things had physical boxes and were sold shrunkwrapped. He was a designer at Apple in the very early days. There were a lot of great products at Macromedia. they had an vector drawing program called Freehand that was better than Illustrator to most people, and they had a video editor called Premiere which was amazingly written by Randy Ubillos who later wrote Final cut for apple! so two of the top 3 video editors were originated by the same guy! A lot of great products got bought and buried when Adobe took over Macromedia. Buy and bury was Microsoft's favorite trick; i once was at a bathroom stall at Comdex with a guy who had built an unbelievable windowed DOS-era multithreaded graphical operating system, needless to say MS bought that quick and killed it off so Windows could launch. It is often much cheaper to buy and bury the competition than to run harder in the race (and that happens in other industries as well). We live in a world of oligopolies, where a few companies control it all. You see it in cloud services, you see it in mobile OS, inkjet printers, memory chips, hard drives, you name it, it usually boils down to just a few big companies. Makes it so hard for small guys to get into the game.

not to be the person always bringing up the same stuff every time (just in the midst of this particular research rabbit hole) but concatenative languages like forth can be very close to the metal and directly support point free programming albeit not in exactly the same way

text:
Hi, all! My name is Nathan Schulzke. I first started programming as a 10-year-old with a copy of Macromedia Flash. Building silly Flash games as a pre-teen doesn't look like much of an educational experience to typical modern educators, but I firmly attribute my success in learning math, logic, and linguistics to early experiments in programming. Because coding was so foundational for me, it has always been surprising and disappointing how few kids get into programming, and how many CS college students find little to no enjoyment in it.

Two summers ago I taught at a coding camp, which proved to be a turning point for me. I was able to observe in others the same transformation that I went through upon learning programming. I watched kids who thought they were dunces realized they could make the computer do _anything_, which gave them hope and confidence that wasn't there before. But I also observed that the programming curriculum we were given actually got in they way of learning, and only the few kids who were able to ignore the curriculum actually learned to love to code.

This observation led me to read everything I could find on teaching programming to kids. I read Seymour Papert, Alan Kay, and Brett Victor, and a philosophy of programming education began to take shape. I began to feel that programming could be used to solve critical educational deficits in a lot of kids, if we can only lift our vision above the C-style programming model and treat computing as the wide and varied creative field it can be. I thought I was alone in this line of thinking until the Hillel Wayne podcast ended up on Hacker News. I'm thrilled to find a group so invested in all of my favorite authors, and am excited to learn more!
